transcription.text: Claude code has five customization layers. Most engineers only touch one. Here is what the other
four unlock and how to know which one you actually need. By the end of this video, you'll have a one
screen mental model of all five layers, a decision tree that picks the right layer for any extension
you want to build, and a map of how all five fire during a single request. This is not a getting
started tutorial. We're not installing anything. We're not showing prompts. We're going one level
above. The architecture. Anthropix docs call this an extension model. The community started calling
it something better. The agent development kit. Five layers. 60 seconds. Layer one is claud.md.
Always on facts that load into every request. Your project constitution. Layer two is skills.
On-demand knowledge. Claude reads a one-line description and pulls the full body only when
relevant. Layer three is hooks. Deterministic guards. Shell commands or HTTP calls that fire
at lifecycle events. 31 events. Five handler types. Layer four is subagents. Forked workers
with their own context window, their own model, their own tools. They return only a summary.
Layer five is plugins. The distribution wrapper. Bundle skills, agents, hooks, and MCP into one
install. Whole team gets the same behavior. An MCP sits next to all five. Not a layer. A sidebar.
The wire to external systems. Each of these solves a different problem. Confuse them and you'll build
the wrong thing. Layer one. Claud.md. It's a markdown file injected as a user message at session
start. Project level. User level. Organization level. They all stack together. Every line loads
into every request. That's the price of being always on. So, Anthropix recommends keeping each
file under 200 lines. Past that, adherence degrades. Claud starts skimming. The mistake
most teams make is treating claud.md as a wiki. Multi-step procedures. Reference documentation.
Edge case notes. All of it crammed in. That content still loads on requests that don't need
it. So, you pay the token cost on every turn for documentation you only use once a week. The right
model is a constitution. Project layout. Build commands. Naming conventions. Never do x rules.
Always do y rules. Things that apply to every session. If claud makes the same mistake twice,
that goes in claud.md. If you find yourself pasting the same prompt over and over,
that's not a claud.md line. That's a skill. New in claud code version two. The instructions loaded
hook tells you which claud.md files actually loaded for a session. And auto memory now writes
its own memory.md file under tilde slash dot claud. So, context survives across conversations.
Layer two and layer three. They look similar. They are not. Skills are guidance. Hooks are
enforcement. A skill is a directory with a skill.md file. YAML front matter on top. Markdown body
underneath. Only the description loads at session start. The body loads on demand when claud matches
the description to your request. Or when you invoke it explicitly with slash and the skill name.
Description budget is 1536 characters per skill. About one percent of your context window in total.
That's how you ship 100 skills without bankrupting context. The full body only enters context when
claud actually needs it. A hook is different. It runs deterministically at a life cycle event.
There are 31 of those events in claud code version two. Session start. User prompt submit. Pre-tool
use. Post-tool use. Stop. And 26 others. Five handler types. Shell command. HTTP call. MCP tool.
Prompt. Subagent. The key difference. Writing never edit dot env in a skill is a request.
Claud can reason around it. A pre-tool use hook that exits with code two when the path matches dot
env. That's a guarantee. It cannot be reasoned around. Skills can also fork into their own
context window when they're heavy. Set context fork in the front matter. Now your skill runs
in an isolated subagent and never pollutes the main thread. Skills and hooks compose. A post-tool
use hook runs lint after every edit. If lint fails, the error feeds back into claud. A slash
fix lint skill then tells claud how to remediate. Enforcement plus guidance. Working together. Layer
four. Subagents. This is where context isolation enters the kit. A subagent is a markdown file at
dot claud slash agents slash some name. Front matter says what model, what tools, what permissions.
The body is a custom system prompt. When the main conversation asks for something matching
the subagent's description, claud forks. The subagent gets its own context window,
its own model, its own tool list, its own permission mode. It does the work. Then it
returns only a summary back to the main thread. This solves two problems at once. First, context
pollution. If you need to read 50 files to answer one question, you don't want all 50 files in your
main conversation. The subagent reads them in its own window. It returns three sentences. Second,
cost routing. The main thread can be opus. The subagent that reads those 50 files can be haiku.
Same answer. Different bill. Three subagents ship built in. Explore. Haiku. Read only. Fast code
based search. Plan. Inherits the model. Read only. Used in plan mode. And general purpose.
Inherits everything. You can also run subagents in isolation work tree mode. The subagent gets
a temporary get work tree. A real copy of the repo isolated from your working tree. It can edit,
test, commit. Nothing touches your branch until you choose to merge. No recursion, by the way.
Subagents cannot spawn other subagents. That cap is intentional. Layer five. Plugins. The wrapper
that turns layer one through four into a deliverable. A plugin is a directory with a
plugin.json manifest. Inside, you can put any combination of skills. Subagents. Hooks. MCP
servers. LSP servers. Custom commands. One install command. Whole team gets the same configuration.
Namespaced. Skills inside a plugin become slash plugin name colon skill name automatically.
So short names don't collide across plugins. Marketplaces are how plugins travel.

tree. Five questions. Pick the first yes. Question one. Should this load on every request?
Yes. Clod.md. Question two. Does this connect to an external system? Postgres. Slack. GitHub.
Figma. Yes. MCP. Note that's the sidebar. Not a layer. Question three. Must this run

None of the above. Default. Skill. And if you want all of that to travel with your team, wrap

the right layer. The sixth question moves it to the team. Here's how the kit fires on a single
request. Clod.md is there from the first token. Always. The user prompt comes in. The user prompt
submit hook fires. Clod scans skill descriptions. If one matches, the skill body loads. Clod reaches
for a tool. The pre-tool use hook fires. Tool runs. The post-tool use hook fires. If the task
needs isolated context, Clod forks a subagent. The subagent does its work in its own window.
Returns a summary. Main thread continues. Plugins wrap whichever of these pieces you bundled,
so they all show up at install time. That's the kit. Five layers. One sidebar. One distribution
mechanism. Most engineers really do touch only layer one. The other four are where production
agents are built. If you want to learn more about AI, check out the dynamis.ai community.
